senses.definition: The armpit; the hollow under the arm where it joins the body.
examples: He felt a sharp pain in his left ascella after lifting the heavy box.
The nurse checked for swollen glands in the patient's ascella.

senses.definition: Plural of ascella; armpits.
examples: The doctor inspected both ascelli for signs of infection.
Sweat patches were visible under his shirt at the ascelli.

senses.definition: A genus of small aquatic crustaceans found in fresh water.
examples: The biologist collected several ascellus specimens from the stream.
Ascella and related genera like Asellus are common in freshwater ecology studies.

senses.definition: To go up or move upward, especially by walking, climbing, or rising.
examples: We slowly ascended the steep mountain path.
The hot air balloon ascended into the clear morning sky.

senses.definition: To reach a higher rank, level, or position.
examples: She ascended to the role of chief executive in just five years.
The party quickly ascended to power after the election.

senses.definition: Able to be gone up or climbed.
examples: The ridge is only ascendable in good weather.
The rock face looked barely ascendable without special equipment.

senses.definition: A state in which someone or something has more power, influence, or control than others.
examples: The company’s rapid ascendance in the tech industry surprised its competitors.
In recent years, renewable energy has been in ascendance.

senses.definition: Plural of ascendance; times or situations when someone or something is dominant.
examples: The book describes the various ascandances and declines of the empire.
Different political parties have enjoyed their ascandances over the decades.

senses.definition: Plural of ascendancy; periods or situations of having power, control, or influence over others.
examples: The novel traces the different ascendancies of rival families.
Empires rise and fall through cycles of ascendancies and defeats.

senses.definition: A position of power, control, or strong influence over someone or something.
examples: The reformers gained ascendancy within the party.
For several decades, the industry enjoyed near-complete ascendancy.

senses.definition: Having more power, influence, or importance than others.
examples: The ascendant faction reshaped the organization’s strategy.
Her ideas are now ascendant in academic circles.

senses.definition: Moving or pointing upward.
examples: An ascendant trend in share prices encouraged investors.
The plant has an ascendant stem that bends toward the light.

senses.definition: A position of greater power or influence compared to others.
examples: With his rivals in the ascendant, he soon lost his job.
Her school’s music program is clearly in the ascendant.

senses.definition: In a way that is rising or becoming more powerful or important.
examples: The party moved ascendantly through the polls in the final weeks.
Her influence grew ascendantly during the negotiations.

senses.definition: People from whom someone is descended, especially earlier generations of a family.
examples: He researched his ascendants back to the eighteenth century.
Their legal rights came through their ascendants.

senses.definition: Past simple and past participle of ascend; went up or rose.
examples: They ascended the narrow staircase in silence.
The plane ascended quickly above the clouds.

senses.definition: The state or process of becoming more powerful or important.
examples: The film depicts the hero’s slow ascendence from poverty to wealth.
The ascendence of digital media has changed advertising.

senses.definition: Plural of ascendence; several rises in power or importance.
examples: The book examines the ascendences and declines of several dynasties.
Over the years, they experienced multiple ascendences in the market.

senses.definition: Plural of ascendency; positions of control or dominating influence.
examples: Different social groups held ascendencies at different times.
The article describes the various ascendencies of global powers.

senses.definition: A position of power, influence, or control over people or events.
examples: The party’s ascendency was clear after the election.
His ascendency in the organization did not last long.

senses.definition: Having more power or influence; dominant.
examples: The ascendent group pushed through major reforms.
Her ascendent position in the company gave her more freedom.

senses.definition: Someone from whom a person is descended; an ancestor.
examples: He spoke proudly of his noble ascendents.
Property rights were transferred through his ascendents.

senses.definition: Plural of ascendent; ancestors.
examples: She traced her ascendents back through church records.
Their rights come from their ascendents under the old law.

senses.definition: A person or thing that goes up or climbs.
examples: Each ascender in the group checked their safety gear carefully.
The experienced ascender led the team up the cliff.

senses.definition: In typography, the part of a lower-case letter that rises above the main body of the letter.
examples: The font has very tall ascenders, which affect readability.
Letters like 'b' and 'd' have ascenders.

senses.definition: Plural of ascender; people or things that climb or move upward.
examples: The ascenders moved slowly up the icy slope.
All ascenders must wear helmets during the climb.

senses.definition: Plural of ascender in typography; parts of letters that extend above the main body.
examples: The font’s tall ascenders make lines look crowded.
Designers adjusted the ascenders to improve legibility.

senses.definition: A mechanical device used by climbers to move upward on a rope.
examples: She checked that her ascendeur was securely attached to the rope.
Using an ascendeur makes long rope climbs safer and more efficient.

senses.definition: Plural of ascendeur; mechanical devices used for rope climbing.
examples: The equipment list includes ropes, harnesses, and two ascendeurs per climber.
All ascendeurs must be inspected before use.

senses.definition: Able to be climbed or gone up.
examples: The peak is only ascendible during the dry season.
Without ropes, the cliff is barely ascendible.

senses.definition: Going up or increasing in size, level, or amount.
examples: The hikers followed an ascending path through the forest.
Arrange the numbers in ascending order.

senses.definition: Present participle of ascend; going up or climbing.
examples: They were slowly ascending the last part of the climb.
Smoke was ascending from the chimney.

senses.definition: In an upward or increasing order or direction.
examples: The data were arranged ascendingly by value.
Rank the candidates ascendingly according to their scores.

senses.definition: Third person singular of ascend; goes up or climbs.
examples: The path ascends sharply after the river.
Smoke ascends from the chimneys on cold mornings.

senses.definition: A lift or elevator in a building (borrowed from French, used mainly in names or technical contexts).
examples: The old hotel still had its original ascenseur.
Please take the ascenseur to the fifth floor.

senses.definition: The act of rising to a higher position, level, or rank.
examples: Her ascension to the leadership of the company was unexpected.
The film tells the story of his ascension from poverty to fame.

senses.definition: In Christian belief, the going up of Jesus Christ into heaven after his resurrection.
examples: The Ascension is celebrated forty days after Easter.
The painting shows Christ’s ascension into heaven.

senses.definition: Relating to rising or moving upward.
examples: The balloon’s ascensional movement was steady and controlled.
The report described the ascensional trend in housing prices.

senses.definition: A climber who attempts or makes an ascent, especially of a mountain or rock face.
examples: The young ascensionist planned a new route up the north face.
Each ascensionist must sign in at the base camp.

senses.definition: Plural of ascensionist; climbers making an ascent.
examples: The ascensionists gathered to discuss the safest route.
All ascensionists must carry proper safety equipment.

senses.definition: Plural of ascension; acts or processes of rising to a higher level or position.
examples: The book explores the ascensions and falls of several leaders.
These economic ascensions were followed by sudden crashes.

senses.definition: The period around Ascension Day in the Christian church year.
examples: The choir prepared special music for Ascensiontide.
During Ascensiontide, the church held evening services.

senses.definition: Tending to rise or move upward.
examples: The balloon’s ascensive motion was gentle.
He studied the ascensive forces in the atmosphere.

senses.definition: A lift or elevator, especially in Spanish‑speaking or South American contexts.
examples: The old ascensor carried residents up the hillside.
Tourists lined up to ride the historic ascensor.

senses.definition: The act of going up or climbing to a higher place.
examples: The final ascent to the summit took three hours.
The plane began its ascent shortly after takeoff.

senses.definition: A path, road, or slope that goes up.
examples: The ascent was narrow but well maintained.
We followed a gentle ascent through the valley.

senses.definition: A process of moving to a more important or higher position.
examples: His rapid ascent in the company surprised everyone.
The party’s ascent to power changed national policy.

senses.definition: Plural of ascent; climbs or upward journeys.
examples: The guide planned several short ascents for the tour.
Their early ascents prepared them for the final summit attempt.

senses.definition: To find out the true or correct information about something.
examples: The police are trying to ascertain what really happened.
We must first ascertain whether the data are reliable.

senses.definition: The quality of being capable of being found out or determined.
examples: The ascertainability of the facts was central to the court’s decision.
They debated the ascertainability of future losses.

senses.definition: Able to be found out or clearly determined.
examples: The total cost is easily ascertainable from the records.
There was no ascertainable reason for the failure.

senses.definition: The state of being capable of being known or determined.
examples: The ascertainableness of the damage was questioned.
They discussed the ascertainableness of the results.

senses.definition: In a way that can be clearly known or determined.
examples: The losses were ascertainably linked to the breach of contract.
No ascertainably reliable data were available.

senses.definition: Past simple and past participle of ascertain; found out something with certainty.
examples: Investigators ascertained that the fire started in the kitchen.
We have not yet ascertained the exact cost.

senses.definition: A person or thing that finds out or determines something.
examples: The judge acts as an objective ascertainer of the facts.
In this process, the auditor is the ascertainer of financial truth.

senses.definition: Present participle of ascertain; finding out something with certainty.
examples: They are still ascertaining the cause of the problem.
The study focuses on ascertaining customer needs.

senses.definition: The act of finding out or establishing something with certainty.
examples: Accurate ascertainment of the facts is essential in justice.
The report deals with the ascertainment of costs.

senses.definition: Plural of ascertainment; several acts of finding out or determining something.
examples: Their various ascertainments led to the final conclusion.
The study’s ascertainments were later questioned.

senses.definition: Third person singular of ascertain; finds out or determines something.
examples: The committee ascertains whether the rules have been followed.
The test ascertains how much the students have learned.
